title: My Best Senior Engineer Quit Last Month.
byline: Devrim Ozcay | Scaling Systems & Surviving Outages

content_html:
My Best Senior Engineer Quit Last Month. Her Exit Interview Was Scheduled for Forty Minutes. The Last Five Changed How I Run My Team.

She was not burned out from writing code. She had barely written any in eight months. She was burned out from reading it. From being the last human standing between an agent’s confident output and our production systems. I did not understand what that did to her until she told me, on her way out the door.

11 min read

Jun 19, 2026

--

Before you read this story, ask yourself:

If AI wrote every line of code you produced tomorrow, would you still be the person people call when production is on fire?

If the answer is uncertain, this story may matter more than you expect.

Priya gave notice on a Tuesday.

She had been with us almost five years. She owned our payments path, the part of the system nobody else fully understood and nobody wanted to. She was the engineer I called first when something was on fire and the one I called last when I needed someone to tell me a deadline was a bad idea. When she said she was leaving, my stomach dropped in the specific way it does when you realize a load-bearing wall just announced it is leaving the building.

The exit interview was on the calendar for forty minutes. I ran the standard playbook. Compensation, was it competitive. Growth path, did she feel stuck. Management, was there something I did. Team, culture, the usual.

For thirty-five minutes she gave me reasonable, professional, slightly generous answers. Comp was fine. She liked the team. I was a decent manager, she said, which was kind and probably half true. Nothing actionable. Nothing I could take to my own boss and point to.

Then, in the last five minutes, with her laptop already half closed, I asked the off-the-record version. What is the actual reason.

And she said something I have been writing down in different forms ever since.

She said: “I haven’t built anything in eight months. I’ve spent eight months reading things a machine wrote, trying to find the bug it was too confident to see. I’m not an engineer here anymore. I’m a verification layer for an agent. I didn’t spend ten years getting good at this to become the thing that stands between a model and the incident it’s about to cause. I’m tired in a way that has nothing to do with hours.”

I wrote it down word for word in my phone after she left. I have read it more times than I want to admit.

The job I did not notice I had changed

Here is the part I am not proud of. I did not see it happening, because from where I sat, everything looked better than it ever had.

When we rolled out coding agents across the team, the metrics went vertical. Velocity up. PRs merging faster. Cycle time down. Throughput up and to the right on every dashboard I showed leadership. I genuinely believed I was watching my team get more powerful. I told them so. I put it in a deck.

What I could not see from the dashboard was where the work had gone. It had not disappeared. It had moved. The agents took over the generation, the part that used to be most of the job, and all of that work flowed downstream and pooled in one place. The review queue. And the review queue had a name on it, over and over again, because she was the only one senior enough to catch what the agent missed.

Priya’s day had quietly transformed. A year earlier she spent it designing systems, writing the hard parts, owning the architecture. By the end she spent it reading. Reading PRs she did not write. Reading agent output that looked clean and was sometimes, in small, vicious, hard-to-spot ways, wrong. Reading more of it every week, because the agents got faster and the volume kept climbing, while her hours stayed exactly the same as they had always been.

The industry has numbers for this now. Review time under heavy AI adoption has climbed nearly 200 percent. Eighty-six percent of engineering leaders report their senior engineers spending more time fixing code. The agents generate at the bottom of the org and the debt collects at the top, on the desk of the one person experienced enough to be trusted with it. I had built that exact funnel without ever deciding to, and Priya was standing at the narrow end of it.

Then the review came in.

“I’ve botched a number of tech interviews not because I’m not smart, but because there really is a flow that should be handled that is a lot easier once you realize what it is.”

Or you can read this interview article

Why reading is so much heavier than writing

I used to think reading code was the easy half. You write the hard half and reviewing is the cooldown. After Priya left I understood I had it backwards, at least now.

Writing code, even hard code, has a rhythm to it. You are building. There is a thing in your head and you are making it real, and there is a quiet satisfaction in watching it take shape, and at the end there is something that is yours. The work is generative and it gives something back.

Reading code that a machine wrote, hunting for the failure it could not see, is the opposite of generative. You produce nothing. You build nothing that is yours. You are scanning, all day, for the one missing retry, the one unhandled null, the one auth check that exists on three endpoints and not the fourth, knowing that if you miss it, it is your name on the incident, not the agent’s. You carry all of the responsibility and none of the creation. And the volume never stops, because the thing producing the work does not get tired, take lunch, or sleep, and you do.

That is not a lighter job. It is a heavier one, stripped of the part that made the weight bearable. Priya was not exhausted from overwork in the hours sense. She was exhausted from doing the least rewarding version of her job, all day, as the only safety net, for eight months, while her dashboard told me she was thriving.

The signal that does not show up on any dashboard

This is the part that should bother every manager reading this, because I lived it and I still almost missed it.

There is no metric for what happened to Priya. Her velocity was fine. Her review throughput was high, that was the whole problem. She did not miss deadlines. She did not complain in one-on-ones, because “I am tired in a way that has nothing to do with hours” is not a sentence that fits in a status update, and because senior engineers are, almost by definition, people who absorb load quietly until they cannot.

By the time the signal reaches you, it is not a warning. It is a resignation. The dashboards that told me my team was thriving were measuring the exact activity that was burning out my best person, and there was no instrument anywhere in my stack pointed at the thing that actually mattered, which was whether the human carrying the verification load could keep carrying it.

And the cost of being wrong about that is brutal in a way leadership underestimates. The replacement cost of a senior engineer in 2026 runs 150,000 to 300,000 dollars when you count recruiting, ramp, and lost institutional knowledge. But that number undersells it, because when Priya left I did not just lose a headcount. I lost the only person who understood the payments path, the verification capacity that was already my bottleneck, and the years of scar tissue that let her catch the agent’s mistakes in the first place. I lost the debt and the only person who could pay it down, in one Tuesday.

What I changed

I am not going to pretend I have this solved. I am six months into figuring it out. But here is what I changed, because someone reading this still has their Priya, and still has time.

I stopped treating review as free. It is not free. It is the most cognitively expensive work on the team now, and it is concentrated on the fewest people. I started measuring review load as deliberately as I measure velocity, and I started spreading it instead of letting it pool on whoever was most senior.

I capped the volume. A 400-line agent PR reviewed by an exhausted senior in eleven minutes is not review, it is a rubber stamp on a future incident. Smaller PRs, fewer of them, real scrutiny on each. The velocity dashboard looks slightly worse. The team is in better shape underneath it.

I gave my seniors back the generative work on purpose. Not because the agent cannot do it, but because a senior engineer who spends one hundred percent of their time reading machine output will leave, and a senior engineer who still gets to build the hard parts will stay. The building was never just output. It was the thing that made the rest of the weight survivable. I had let the agents take it without noticing it was load-bearing for the human, not just the codebase.

And I started asking the off-the-record question in one-on-ones, months before the exit interview, when there is still time to do something with the answer. “Is the work still the work you want to be doing.” Most of the time the answer is fine. The one time it is not, I would rather hear it across a desk in March than in a resignation letter in October.

What I wish I had known

If you manage engineers in 2026 and your velocity dashboards have never looked better, I am not telling you the dashboards are lying. They are telling the truth about throughput. They are simply silent about the thing that will actually cost you your best people, which is what the new shape of the work is doing to the humans carrying its heaviest, least rewarding part.

Priya was not failing. She was the strongest engineer I had, doing the hardest version of the job, with no instrument anywhere pointed at the strain, until the strain became a Tuesday.

The agents did not burn her out. The way I let the agents reshape her job did. That one is on me, and it was preventable, and I did not see it because I was looking at the wrong numbers the entire time.

If you have a Priya, and you almost certainly do, the last five minutes of her exit interview are already written. You just get to choose whether you hear them now, while you can still change the ending, or in October, when all you can do is write down what she says and read it too many times afterward.

After Priya left, I started writing down the patterns I kept seeing.

The incident mistakes that repeated. The architecture decisions that looked different on the surface but shared the same underlying tradeoffs. The review habits that turned some senior engineers into bottlenecks and helped others scale their influence.

Mostly for myself at first.

Three months after Priya left, I tried what half the comments told me to do: I let AI review the AI. It looked like the answer, until the night it nearly shipped a completely broken system with every test green. I wrote about what happened, and what I understand now, here: Three Months After Priya Left, We Let AI Review the AI.

The next outage is coming whether you’re ready or not.

Grab the free field kit and keep it open before the page arrives:

Priya wasn’t the first senior engineer I’ve seen become a review bottleneck.

After enough incidents, architecture mistakes, and production failures, I started documenting the patterns that kept repeating.

After Priya left, I started writing things down.

Not to publish. Not to sell. Because the patterns were repeating and I needed somewhere to put them so I could actually see them.

The incident that looked unique but was the same connection pool failure we had eighteen months earlier. The architecture debate that ran for two weeks and would have ended in thirty minutes if someone had asked the right constraint question first. The senior engineer in the interview who knew everything and still did not get the offer, for a reason nobody told them.

I had seen all of it before. Most of it more than once. And every time I watched someone pay for a lesson that already existed somewhere, in someone else’s scar tissue, I wrote it down.

Three years of notes. Thirty-seven of them eventually became a library.

It is called The Senior Backend Engineer Handbook. It covers the failure modes behind most production outages, the architecture tradeoffs that compound for years, the production references you actually need at 3 AM, and the communication layer that separates two engineers with identical depth in an interview room.

It is not about Priya. But it is the thing I wish she had had access to, not because it would have saved her from the job I let become something else, but because the judgment it builds is exactly what made her irreplaceable in the first place.

If you are the engineer carrying the load right now, or the manager watching someone carry it, it is here:

The Senior Backend Engineer Handbook: From 3 AM Outages to Senior Offers

And thank you to everyone who took the time to read, think, disagree, agree, and leave a thoughtful comment. The discussion has been far more interesting than I expected.

Every Page In This Library Cost Someone Something.

Nobody pays senior engineers for what they know.

They pay them for what they notice.

The deadlock before it becomes an outage.

The tradeoff before it becomes technical debt.

The interview signal before it becomes a rejection.

Most of the material in this library was written after something went wrong.

A production incident.

A bad decision.

A missed opportunity.

Experience is a brutal teacher.

This is what the lessons looked like after the scars healed.

Best,

Devrim

sentences.zh: 在三十五分钟的时间里，她给了我合理、专业、略显慷慨的答案。

sentences.en: Comp was fine.
sentences.zh: 比较很好。

sentences.en: She liked the team.
sentences.zh: 她喜欢这个团队。

sentences.en: I was a decent manager, she said, which was kind and probably half true.
sentences.zh: “我是一位不错的经理，”她说，这话很善意，或许也有一半是对的。

sentences.en: Nothing actionable.
sentences.zh: 没有什么可操作的。

sentences.en: Nothing I could take to my own boss and point to.
sentences.zh: 我没有什么可以向我自己的老板提出并指出的。

blocks.html_en: Then, in the last five minutes, with her laptop already half closed, I asked the off-the-record version. What is the actual reason.

sentences.en: Then, in the last five minutes, with her laptop already half closed, I asked the off-the-record version.
sentences.zh: 然后，在最后五分钟，在她的笔记本电脑已经半合上的情况下，我询问了非记录版本。

sentences.en: What is the actual reason.
sentences.zh: 真正的原因是什么。

blocks.html_en: And she said something I have been writing down in different forms ever since.

sentences.en: And she said something I have been writing down in different forms ever since.
sentences.zh: 从那时起，她说了一些我一直以不同形式写下来的事情。

blocks.html_en: She said: “I haven’t built anything in eight months. I’ve spent eight months reading things a machine wrote, trying to find the bug it was too confident to see. I’m not an engineer here anymore. I’m a verification layer for an agent. I didn’t spend ten years getting good at this to become the thing that stands between a model and the incident it’s about to cause. I’m tired in a way that has nothing to do with hours.”

sentences.en: She said: “I haven’t built anything in eight months.
sentences.zh: 她说：“我已经八个月没有建造任何东西了。

sentences.en: I’ve spent eight months reading things a machine wrote, trying to find the bug it was too confident to see.
sentences.zh: 我花了八个月的时间阅读机器写的东西，试图找到它过于自信而看不到的错误。

sentences.en: I’m not an engineer here anymore.
sentences.zh: 我不再是这里的工程师了。

sentences.en: I’m a verification layer for an agent.
sentences.zh: 我是代理的验证层。

sentences.en: I didn’t spend ten years getting good at this to become the thing that stands between a model and the incident it’s about to cause.
sentences.zh: 我并没有花费十年的时间来擅长这一点，成为一个介于模型和它即将引发的事件之间的东西。

sentences.en: I’m tired in a way that has nothing to do with hours.”
sentences.zh: 我的累与工作时间无关。”

blocks.html_en: I wrote it down word for word in my phone after she left. I have read it more times than I want to admit.

sentences.en: I wrote it down word for word in my phone after she left.
sentences.zh: 她走后我一字不差地把这句话记在手机里。

sentences.en: I have read it more times than I want to admit.
sentences.zh: 我读过它的次数比我愿意承认的还要多。

blocks.html_en: The job I did not notice I had changed

sentences.en: The job I did not notice I had changed
sentences.zh: 我没有注意到我的工作已经改变

blocks.html_en: Here is the part I am not proud of. I did not see it happening, because from where I sat, everything looked better than it ever had.

sentences.en: Here is the part I am not proud of.
sentences.zh: 这是我不值得骄傲的部分。

sentences.en: I did not see it happening, because from where I sat, everything looked better than it ever had.
sentences.zh: 我没有看到它发生，因为从我坐的地方来看，一切看起来都比以前更好。

blocks.html_en: When we rolled out coding agents across the team, the metrics went vertical. Velocity up. PRs merging faster. Cycle time down. Throughput up and to the right on every dashboard I showed leadership. I genuinely believed I was watching my team get more powerful. I told them so. I put it in a deck.

sentences.en: When we rolled out coding agents across the team, the metrics went vertical.
sentences.zh: 当我们在整个团队中推出编码代理时，指标变得垂直。

sentences.en: Velocity up.
sentences.zh: 速度上来。

sentences.en: PRs merging faster.
sentences.zh: PR 合并速度更快。

sentences.en: Cycle time down.
sentences.zh: 周期时间缩短。

sentences.en: Throughput up and to the right on every dashboard I showed leadership.
sentences.zh: 我在每个仪表板上的吞吐量向上和向右显示了领导力。

sentences.en: I genuinely believed I was watching my team get more powerful.
sentences.zh: 我真诚地相信我正在看着我的团队变得更加强大。

sentences.en: I told them so.
sentences.zh: 我告诉他们了。

sentences.en: I put it in a deck.
sentences.zh: 我把它放在甲板上。

blocks.html_en: What I could not see from the dashboard was where the work had gone. It had not disappeared. It had moved. The agents took over the generation, the part that used to be most of the job, and all of that work flowed downstream and pooled in one place. The review queue. And the review queue had a name on it, over and over again, because she was the only one senior enough to catch what the agent missed.

sentences.en: What I could not see from the dashboard was where the work had gone.
sentences.zh: 我从仪表板上看不到工作已经完成的地方。

sentences.en: It had not disappeared.
sentences.zh: 它并没有消失。

sentences.en: It had moved.
sentences.zh: 它已经移动了。

sentences.en: The agents took over the generation, the part that used to be most of the job, and all of that work flowed downstream and pooled in one place.
sentences.zh: 代理接管了这一代，这部分曾经是大部分工作，所有工作都流向下游并集中在一个地方。

sentences.en: The review queue.
sentences.zh: 审核队列。

sentences.en: And the review queue had a name on it, over and over again, because she was the only one senior enough to catch what the agent missed.
sentences.zh: 审查队列上一遍又一遍地有一个名字，因为她是唯一一个足够资深的人来发现特工错过的东西。

blocks.html_en: Priya’s day had quietly transformed. A year earlier she spent it designing systems, writing the hard parts, owning the architecture. By the end she spent it reading. Reading PRs she did not write. Reading agent output that looked clean and was sometimes, in small, vicious, hard-to-spot ways, wrong. Reading more of it every week, because the agents got faster and the volume kept climbing, while her hours stayed exactly the same as they had always been.

sentences.en: Priya’s day had quietly transformed.
sentences.zh: 普莉亚的一天悄然发生了变化。

sentences.en: A year earlier she spent it designing systems, writing the hard parts, owning the architecture.
sentences.zh: 一年前，她花了很多时间设计系统、编写困难的部分、拥有架构。

sentences.en: By the end she spent it reading.
sentences.zh: 最后她把时间都花在了读书上。

sentences.en: Reading PRs she did not write.
sentences.zh: 阅读不是她写的 PR。

sentences.en: Reading agent output that looked clean and was sometimes, in small, vicious, hard-to-spot ways, wrong.
sentences.zh: 阅读代理的输出看起来很干净，但有时却以微小的、恶意的、难以发现的方式出现了错误。

sentences.en: Reading more of it every week, because the agents got faster and the volume kept climbing, while her hours stayed exactly the same as they had always been.
sentences.zh: 每周都会阅读更多内容，因为特工的速度越来越快，数量不断增加，而她的工作时间却与以前一模一样。

blocks.html_en: The industry has numbers for this now. Review time under heavy AI adoption has climbed nearly 200 percent. Eighty-six percent of engineering leaders report their senior engineers spending more time fixing code. The agents generate at the bottom of the org and the debt collects at the top, on the desk of the one person experienced enough to be trusted with it. I had built that exact funnel without ever deciding to, and Priya was standing at the narrow end of it.

sentences.en: The industry has numbers for this now.
sentences.zh: 目前业界已有这方面的数据。

sentences.en: Review time under heavy AI adoption has climbed nearly 200 percent.
sentences.zh: 大量采用 AI 的情况下，审核时间增长了近 200%。

sentences.en: Eighty-six percent of engineering leaders report their senior engineers spending more time fixing code.
sentences.zh: 百分之八十六的工程领导者表示，他们的高级工程师花费了更多时间修复代码。

sentences.en: The agents generate at the bottom of the org and the debt collects at the top, on the desk of the one person experienced enough to be trusted with it.
sentences.zh: 代理在组织的底层产生，债务在顶层收集，在一个有足够经验、值得信任的人的办公桌上。

sentences.en: I had built that exact funnel without ever deciding to, and Priya was standing at the narrow end of it.
sentences.zh: 我在没有决定的情况下就已经建造了那个精确的漏斗，而普莉亚就站在它的狭窄一端。

blocks.html_en: Then the review came in.

sentences.en: Then the review came in.
sentences.zh: 然后评论就进来了。

blocks.html_en: “I’ve botched a number of tech interviews not because I’m not smart, but because there really is a flow that should be handled that is a lot easier once you realize what it is.”

sentences.en: “I’ve botched a number of tech interviews not because I’m not smart, but because there really is a flow that should be handled that is a lot easier once you realize what it is.”
sentences.zh: “我搞砸了很多技术面试，并不是因为我不聪明，而是因为确实存在一个应该处理的流程，一旦你意识到它是什么，就会容易得多。”

blocks.html_en: Press enter or click to view image in full size

sentences.en: Press enter or click to view image in full size
sentences.zh: 按 Enter 或单击以查看全尺寸图像

blocks.html_en: Or you can read this interview article

sentences.en: Or you can read this interview article
sentences.zh: 或者你可以阅读这篇采访文章

blocks.html_en: Why reading is so much heavier than writing

sentences.en: Why reading is so much heavier than writing
sentences.zh: 为什么阅读比写作重得多

blocks.html_en: I used to think reading code was the easy half. You write the hard half and reviewing is the cooldown. After Priya left I understood I had it backwards, at least now.

sentences.en: I used to think reading code was the easy half.
sentences.zh: 我曾经认为阅读代码是容易的一半。

sentences.en: You write the hard half and reviewing is the cooldown.
sentences.zh: 你写了困难的一半，然后复习就是冷却时间。

sentences.en: After Priya left I understood I had it backwards, at least now.
sentences.zh: 普莉亚离开后，我明白我已经倒退了，至少现在是这样。

blocks.html_en: Writing code, even hard code, has a rhythm to it. You are building. There is a thing in your head and you are making it real, and there is a quiet satisfaction in watching it take shape, and at the end there is something that is yours. The work is generative and it gives something back.

sentences.en: Writing code, even hard code, has a rhythm to it.
sentences.zh: 编写代码，即使是硬代码，也有节奏。

sentences.en: You are building.
sentences.zh: 你正在建造。

sentences.en: There is a thing in your head and you are making it real, and there is a quiet satisfaction in watching it take shape, and at the end there is something that is yours.
sentences.zh: 你脑子里有一个东西，你正在把它变成现实，看着它成形时会有一种安静的满足感，最后有一些东西是你的。

sentences.en: The work is generative and it gives something back.
sentences.zh: 这项工作是富有创造力的，它会有所回报。

blocks.html_en: Reading code that a machine wrote, hunting for the failure it could not see, is the opposite of generative. You produce nothing. You build nothing that is yours. You are scanning, all day, for the one missing retry, the one unhandled null, the one auth check that exists on three endpoints and not the fourth, knowing that if you miss it, it is your name on the incident, not the agent’s. You carry all of the responsibility and none of the creation. And the volume never stops, because the thing producing the work does not get tired, take lunch, or sleep, and you do.

sentences.en: Reading code that a machine wrote, hunting for the failure it could not see, is the opposite of generative.
sentences.zh: 阅读机器编写的代码，寻找它看不到的故障，与生成相反。

sentences.en: You produce nothing.
sentences.zh: 你什么也没生产。

sentences.en: You build nothing that is yours.
sentences.zh: 你没有建造任何属于你的东西。

sentences.en: You are scanning, all day, for the one missing retry, the one unhandled null, the one auth check that exists on three endpoints and not the fourth, knowing that if you miss it, it is your name on the incident, not the agent’s.
sentences.zh: 您一整天都在扫描一次丢失的重试、一次未处理的 null、一次存在于三个端点（而不是第四个端点）上的身份验证检查，因为您知道，如果您错过了它，那是您在事件中的名字，而不是代理的名字。

sentences.en: You carry all of the responsibility and none of the creation.
sentences.zh: 你承担所有的责任，但不承担任何创造。

sentences.en: And the volume never stops, because the thing producing the work does not get tired, take lunch, or sleep, and you do.
sentences.zh: 而且音量永远不会停止，因为产生工作的东西不会累，吃午饭或睡觉，而你会累。

blocks.html_en: That is not a lighter job. It is a heavier one, stripped of the part that made the weight bearable. Priya was not exhausted from overwork in the hours sense. She was exhausted from doing the least rewarding version of her job, all day, as the only safety net, for eight months, while her dashboard told me she was thriving.

sentences.en: That is not a lighter job.
sentences.zh: 这可不是一件轻松的工作。

sentences.en: It is a heavier one, stripped of the part that made the weight bearable.
sentences.zh: 它比较重，去掉了可以承受重量的部分。

sentences.en: Priya was not exhausted from overwork in the hours sense.
sentences.zh: 从时间意义上来说，普莉亚并没有因为过度劳累而疲惫不堪。

sentences.en: She was exhausted from doing the least rewarding version of her job, all day, as the only safety net, for eight months, while her dashboard told me she was thriving.
sentences.zh: 八个月来，她整天做着最没有回报的工作，作为唯一的安全网，她已经筋疲力尽，而她的仪表板告诉我，她正在蓬勃发展。

blocks.html_en: The signal that does not show up on any dashboard

sentences.en: The signal that does not show up on any dashboard
sentences.zh: 任何仪表板上均未显示的信号

blocks.html_en: This is the part that should bother every manager reading this, because I lived it and I still almost missed it.

sentences.en: This is the part that should bother every manager reading this, because I lived it and I still almost missed it.
sentences.zh: 这是每个读到这篇文章的经理都会感到困扰的部分，因为我经历过它，但我仍然几乎错过了它。

blocks.html_en: There is no metric for what happened to Priya. Her velocity was fine. Her review throughput was high, that was the whole problem. She did not miss deadlines. She did not complain in one-on-ones, because “I am tired in a way that has nothing to do with hours” is not a sentence that fits in a status update, and because senior engineers are, almost by definition, people who absorb load quietly until they cannot.

sentences.en: There is no metric for what happened to Priya.
sentences.zh: 普里亚发生的事情没有任何衡量标准。

sentences.en: Her velocity was fine.
sentences.zh: 她的速度很好。

sentences.en: Her review throughput was high, that was the whole problem.
sentences.zh: 她的审核吞吐量很高，这就是问题所在。

sentences.en: She did not miss deadlines.
sentences.zh: 她没有错过最后期限。

sentences.en: She did not complain in one-on-ones, because “I am tired in a way that has nothing to do with hours” is not a sentence that fits in a status update, and because senior engineers are, almost by definition, people who absorb load quietly until they cannot.
sentences.zh: 她没有一对一地抱怨，因为“我很累，与工作时间无关”这句话不适合状态更新，而且因为几乎从定义上看，高级工程师就是那些安静地承受负荷直到无法承受的人。

blocks.html_en: By the time the signal reaches you, it is not a warning. It is a resignation. The dashboards that told me my team was thriving were measuring the exact activity that was burning out my best person, and there was no instrument anywhere in my stack pointed at the thing that actually mattered, which was whether the human carrying the verification load could keep carrying it.

sentences.en: By the time the signal reaches you, it is not a warning.
sentences.zh: 当信号到达您时，这并不是警告。

sentences.en: It is a resignation.
sentences.zh: 这是辞职。

sentences.en: The dashboards that told me my team was thriving were measuring the exact activity that was burning out my best person, and there was no instrument anywhere in my stack pointed at the thing that actually mattered, which was whether the human carrying the verification load could keep carrying it.
sentences.zh: 告诉我我的团队正在蓬勃发展的仪表板正在测量那些让我最优秀的人员精疲力尽的活动，而我的堆栈中没有任何仪器指向真正重要的事情，即承载验证负载的人员是否可以继续承载它。

blocks.html_en: And the cost of being wrong about that is brutal in a way leadership underestimates. The replacement cost of a senior engineer in 2026 runs 150,000 to 300,000 dollars when you count recruiting, ramp, and lost institutional knowledge. But that number undersells it, because when Priya left I did not just lose a headcount. I lost the only person who understood the payments path, the verification capacity that was already my bottleneck, and the years of scar tissue that let her catch the agent’s mistakes in the first place. I lost the debt and the only person who could pay it down, in one Tuesday.

sentences.en: And the cost of being wrong about that is brutal in a way leadership underestimates.
sentences.zh: 领导层低估了错误的代价是残酷的。

sentences.en: The replacement cost of a senior engineer in 2026 runs 150,000 to 300,000 dollars when you count recruiting, ramp, and lost institutional knowledge.
sentences.zh: 如果算上招聘、升级和机构知识流失，2026 年高级工程师的替代成本将达到 15 万至 30 万美元。

sentences.en: But that number undersells it, because when Priya left I did not just lose a headcount.
sentences.zh: 但这个数字低估了它的价值，因为当 Priya 离开时，我不仅失去了员工。

sentences.en: I lost the only person who understood the payments path, the verification capacity that was already my bottleneck, and the years of scar tissue that let her catch the agent’s mistakes in the first place.
sentences.zh: 我失去了唯一一个了解支付路径的人，失去了已经成为我瓶颈的验证能力，以及让她首先发现代理人错误的多年疤痕组织。

sentences.en: I lost the debt and the only person who could pay it down, in one Tuesday.
sentences.zh: 在一个星期二的时间里，我失去了债务，也是唯一一个有能力还清债务的人。

blocks.html_en: What I changed

sentences.en: What I changed
sentences.zh: 我改变了什么

blocks.html_en: I am not going to pretend I have this solved. I am six months into figuring it out. But here is what I changed, because someone reading this still has their Priya, and still has time.

sentences.en: I am not going to pretend I have this solved.
sentences.zh: 我不会假装我已经解决了这个问题。

sentences.en: I am six months into figuring it out.
sentences.zh: 我花了六个月的时间才弄清楚。

sentences.en: But here is what I changed, because someone reading this still has their Priya, and still has time.
sentences.zh: 但这是我改变的，因为读这篇文章的人仍然有他们的 Priya，并且仍然有时间。

blocks.html_en: I stopped treating review as free. It is not free. It is the most cognitively expensive work on the team now, and it is concentrated on the fewest people. I started measuring review load as deliberately as I measure velocity, and I started spreading it instead of letting it pool on whoever was most senior.

sentences.en: I stopped treating review as free.
sentences.zh: 我不再将评论视为免费。

sentences.en: It is not free.
sentences.zh: 它不是免费的。

sentences.en: It is the most cognitively expensive work on the team now, and it is concentrated on the fewest people.
sentences.zh: 这是目前团队中认知成本最高的工作，而且集中在最少的人身上。

sentences.en: I started measuring review load as deliberately as I measure velocity, and I started spreading it instead of letting it pool on whoever was most senior.
sentences.zh: 我开始像衡量速度一样谨慎地衡量审稿量，并且开始将其分散，而不是让其集中在最资深的人身上。

sentences.en: Get Devrim Ozcay | Scaling Systems & Surviving Outages’s stories in your inbox
sentences.zh: 获取德夫里姆·奥兹凯 |收件箱中的扩展系统和幸存中断的故事

sentences.en: Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.
sentences.zh: 免费加入 Medium 以获取作者的最新动态。

sentences.en: Remember me for faster sign in
sentences.zh: 记住我以便更快登录

blocks.html_en: Remember me for faster sign in

sentences.en: Remember me for faster sign in
sentences.zh: 记住我以便更快登录

blocks.html_en: I capped the volume. A 400-line agent PR reviewed by an exhausted senior in eleven minutes is not review, it is a rubber stamp on a future incident. Smaller PRs, fewer of them, real scrutiny on each. The velocity dashboard looks slightly worse. The team is in better shape underneath it.

sentences.en: I capped the volume.
sentences.zh: 我限制了音量。

sentences.en: A 400-line agent PR reviewed by an exhausted senior in eleven minutes is not review, it is a rubber stamp on a future incident.
sentences.zh: 一位精疲力竭的前辈在 11 分钟内审阅的 400 行代理 PR 并不是审阅，而是未来事件的橡皮图章。

sentences.en: Smaller PRs, fewer of them, real scrutiny on each.
sentences.zh: PR 较小，数量较少，对每个都进行真正的审查。

sentences.en: The velocity dashboard looks slightly worse.
sentences.zh: 速度仪表板看起来稍差一些。

sentences.en: The team is in better shape underneath it.
sentences.zh: 球队在其之下的状态更好。

blocks.html_en: I gave my seniors back the generative work on purpose. Not because the agent cannot do it, but because a senior engineer who spends one hundred percent of their time reading machine output will leave, and a senior engineer who still gets to build the hard parts will stay. The building was never just output. It was the thing that made the rest of the weight survivable. I had let the agents take it without noticing it was load-bearing for the human, not just the codebase.

sentences.en: I gave my seniors back the generative work on purpose.
sentences.zh: 我故意把创造性的工作交还给我的前辈。

sentences.en: Not because the agent cannot do it, but because a senior engineer who spends one hundred percent of their time reading machine output will leave, and a senior engineer who still gets to build the hard parts will stay.
sentences.zh: 不是因为代理无法做到这一点，而是因为花费 100% 时间读取机器输出的高级工程师将会离开，而仍然能够构建硬部件的高级工程师将会留下来。

sentences.en: The building was never just output.
sentences.zh: 建筑从来不只是输出。

sentences.en: It was the thing that made the rest of the weight survivable.
sentences.zh: 正是这个东西让其余的重量得以幸存。

content_html_zh:
我最好的高级工程师上个月辞职了。 她的离职面谈安排了四十分钟。 过去五年改变了我管理团队的方式。

她并没有因编写代码而精疲力竭。 八个月来她几乎没有写过任何东西。 她读完后精疲力尽。 从成为代理的自信输出和我们的生产系统之间的最后一个人。 我不明白这对她造成了什么影响，直到她出门时告诉我。

阅读时间 11 分钟 2026 年 6 月 19 日

--

在阅读这个故事之前，先问问自己：

如果人工智能明天编写你生产的每一行代码，当生产火热时人们还会打电话给你吗？

如果答案不确定，这个故事可能比你想象的更重要。

Priya 在周二发出了通知。

她和我们在一起快五年了。 她拥有我们的支付路径，这是系统的一部分，没有人完全理解，也没有人愿意。 当事情着火时，我第一个打电话给她，当我需要有人告诉我截止日期是个坏主意时，我最后打电话给她。 当她说她要离开时，我的胃像当你意识到承重墙刚刚宣布它要离开大楼时那样下降。

离职面谈在日历上持续了四十分钟。 我运行了标准剧本。 薪酬，是否有竞争力。 成长的道路，她有没有感觉被卡住了。 管理层，我做了什么吗？ 团队、文化，都是平常的。

在三十五分钟的时间里，她给了我合理、专业、略显慷慨的答案。 比较很好。 她喜欢这个团队。 “我是一位不错的经理，”她说，这话很善意，或许也有一半是对的。 没有什么可操作的。 我没有什么可以向我自己的老板提出并指出的。

然后，在最后五分钟，在她的笔记本电脑已经半合上的情况下，我询问了非记录版本。 真正的原因是什么。

从那时起，她说了一些我一直以不同形式写下来的事情。

她说：“我已经八个月没有建造任何东西了。 我花了八个月的时间阅读机器写的东西，试图找到它过于自信而看不到的错误。 我不再是这里的工程师了。 我是代理的验证层。 我并没有花费十年的时间来擅长这一点，成为一个介于模型和它即将引发的事件之间的东西。 我的累与工作时间无关。”

她走后我一字不差地把这句话记在手机里。 我读过它的次数比我愿意承认的还要多。

我没有注意到我的工作已经改变

这是我不值得骄傲的部分。 我没有看到它发生，因为从我坐的地方来看，一切看起来都比以前更好。

当我们在整个团队中推出编码代理时，指标变得垂直。 速度上来。 PR 合并速度更快。 周期时间缩短。 我在每个仪表板上的吞吐量向上和向右显示了领导力。 我真诚地相信我正在看着我的团队变得更加强大。 我告诉他们了。 我把它放在甲板上。

我从仪表板上看不到工作已经完成的地方。 它并没有消失。 它已经移动了。 代理接管了这一代，这部分曾经是大部分工作，所有工作都流向下游并集中在一个地方。 审核队列。 审查队列上一遍又一遍地有一个名字，因为她是唯一一个足够资深的人来发现特工错过的东西。

普莉亚的一天悄然发生了变化。 一年前，她花了很多时间设计系统、编写困难的部分、拥有架构。 最后她把时间都花在了读书上。 阅读不是她写的 PR。 阅读代理的输出看起来很干净，但有时却以微小的、恶意的、难以发现的方式出现了错误。 每周都会阅读更多内容，因为特工的速度越来越快，数量不断增加，而她的工作时间却与以前一模一样。

目前业界已有这方面的数据。 大量采用 AI 的情况下，审核时间增长了近 200%。 百分之八十六的工程领导者表示，他们的高级工程师花费了更多时间修复代码。 代理在组织的底层产生，债务在顶层收集，在一个有足够经验、值得信任的人的办公桌上。 我在没有决定的情况下就已经建造了那个精确的漏斗，而普莉亚就站在它的狭窄一端。

然后评论就进来了。

“我搞砸了很多技术面试，并不是因为我不聪明，而是因为确实存在一个应该处理的流程，一旦你意识到它是什么，就会容易得多。”

或者你可以阅读这篇采访文章

为什么阅读比写作重得多

我曾经认为阅读代码是容易的一半。 你写了困难的一半，然后复习就是冷却时间。 普莉亚离开后，我明白我已经倒退了，至少现在是这样。

编写代码，即使是硬代码，也有节奏。 你正在建造。 你脑子里有一个东西，你正在把它变成现实，看着它成形时会有一种安静的满足感，最后有一些东西是你的。 这项工作是富有创造力的，它会有所回报。

阅读机器编写的代码，寻找它看不到的故障，与生成相反。 你什么也没生产。 你没有建造任何属于你的东西。 您一整天都在扫描一次丢失的重试、一次未处理的 null、一次存在于三个端点（而不是第四个端点）上的身份验证检查，因为您知道，如果您错过了它，那是您在事件中的名字，而不是代理的名字。 你承担所有的责任，但不承担任何创造。 而且音量永远不会停止，因为产生工作的东西不会累，吃午饭或睡觉，而你会累。

这可不是一件轻松的工作。 它比较重，去掉了可以承受重量的部分。 从时间意义上来说，普莉亚并没有因为过度劳累而疲惫不堪。 八个月来，她整天做着最没有回报的工作，作为唯一的安全网，她已经筋疲力尽，而她的仪表板告诉我，她正在蓬勃发展。

任何仪表板上均未显示的信号

这是每个读到这篇文章的经理都会感到困扰的部分，因为我经历过它，但我仍然几乎错过了它。

普里亚发生的事情没有任何衡量标准。 她的速度很好。 她的审核吞吐量很高，这就是问题所在。 她没有错过最后期限。 她没有一对一地抱怨，因为“我很累，与工作时间无关”这句话不适合状态更新，而且因为几乎从定义上看，高级工程师就是那些安静地承受负荷直到无法承受的人。

当信号到达您时，这并不是警告。 这是辞职。 告诉我我的团队正在蓬勃发展的仪表板正在测量那些让我最优秀的人员精疲力尽的活动，而我的堆栈中没有任何仪器指向真正重要的事情，即承载验证负载的人员是否可以继续承载它。

领导层低估了错误的代价是残酷的。 如果算上招聘、升级和机构知识流失，2026 年高级工程师的替代成本将达到 15 万至 30 万美元。 但这个数字低估了它的价值，因为当 Priya 离开时，我不仅失去了员工。 我失去了唯一一个了解支付路径的人，失去了已经成为我瓶颈的验证能力，以及让她首先发现代理人错误的多年疤痕组织。 在一个星期二的时间里，我失去了债务，也是唯一一个有能力还清债务的人。

我改变了什么

我不会假装我已经解决了这个问题。 我花了六个月的时间才弄清楚。 但这是我改变的，因为读这篇文章的人仍然有他们的 Priya，并且仍然有时间。

我不再将评论视为免费。 它不是免费的。 这是目前团队中认知成本最高的工作，而且集中在最少的人身上。 我开始像衡量速度一样谨慎地衡量审稿量，并且开始将其分散，而不是让其集中在最资深的人身上。

我限制了音量。 一位精疲力竭的前辈在 11 分钟内审阅的 400 行代理 PR 并不是审阅，而是未来事件的橡皮图章。 PR 较小，数量较少，对每个都进行真正的审查。 速度仪表板看起来稍差一些。 球队在其之下的状态更好。

我故意把创造性的工作交还给我的前辈。 不是因为代理无法做到这一点，而是因为花费 100% 时间读取机器输出的高级工程师将会离开，而仍然能够构建硬部件的高级工程师将会留下来。 建筑从来不只是输出。 正是这个东西让其余的重量得以幸存。 我让特工拿走它，却没有注意到它承载着人类，而不仅仅是代码库。

在离职面谈前几个月，我开始一对一地问这个不公开的问题，当时还有时间对答案做一些事情。 “现在的工作还是你想做的工作吗？” 大多数时候答案都很好。 唯一一次不是这样，我宁愿在三月份在办公桌前听到这句话，也不愿在十月份的辞职信中听到。

我希望我知道什么

如果您在 2026 年管理工程师，并且您的速度仪表板看起来从未如此出色，那么我并不是在告诉您仪表板在撒谎。 他们所说的吞吐量是真实的。 他们只是对那些实际上会让你最优秀的员工付出代价的事情保持沉默，而这正是新的工作形式对承担最重、回报最少的部分的人类所做的事情。

Priya 并没有失败。 她是我拥有的最强大的工程师，完成了最困难的工作，没有任何仪器指向压力，直到压力变成星期二。

特工们并没有把她烧死。 我让特工重塑她工作的方式确实做到了。 那个就在我身上，这是可以预防的，但我没有看到它，因为我一直在看错误的数字。

如果你有 Priya（你几乎肯定有），她的离职面谈的最后五分钟已经写好了。 你只需选择是现在听，还是在十月份听，那时你仍然可以改变结局，或者在十月，那时你所能做的就是写下她说的话，然后读很多遍。
